While Dr. Skoury possesses clinical knowledge, I regret to say that his approach to teaching consistently caused significant emotional distress to myself and many of my colleagues. His feedback was frequently delivered in a manner that felt belittling, sarcastic, and unconstructively critical — often in front of patients, nurses, or fellow team members.

The environment created by such interactions was not only psychologically unsafe, but also counterproductive to learning. I often found myself anxious and hesitant to ask questions — not due to a lack of motivation, but out of fear of being humiliated. 


It’s important to note that this feedback is not isolated to me — I’ve witnessed similar behavior directed toward other residents, students, and nursing staff.

As someone who deeply values growth, accountability, and professionalism, I hope this feedback serves as a catalyst for reflection and change. The future of medical education must be built on respect, psychological safety, and mentorship — not fear.
Sometimes it feels like dr skoury has a vendetta against the nurses and at every minor inconvenience, he puts all the blame on the nurses irrespective of what actually happened.

As a senior physician at AUBMC I think leadership sometimes doesn't think about the wellness of the employees at AUBMC as much as it should and puts them under harsh work conditions. Example the construction work, changing the flooring of the ED that happened for 1 week where we were exposed to daily noise pollution that was not coordinated w ED leadership and jeopardized patient care and the well being of the whole ED team as well as the patients' well being. Instead of doing lunch gatherings or distributing candy on doctor or nurses day we should focus on a culture that fosters wellness.
Other than this point which was a major let down I am happy at work and grateful for AUBMC and what it stands for.
